([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Países Bajos Españoles*; [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Spaanse Nederlanden*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Pays-Bas espagnols*; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Spanische Niederlande*; historically in Spanish: *Flandes*, the name "Flanders" was used as a *[pars pro toto](/wiki/Pars_pro_toto "Pars pro toto")*)[[4]](#cite_note-4) were a collection of [States](/wiki/Imperial_States "Imperial States") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), held in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") by the [Spanish Habsburgs](/wiki/Spanish_Habsburgs "Spanish Habsburgs"), but not annexed to the [Spanish Crown](/wiki/Spanish_Crown "Spanish Crown"), thus encompassing the second period in history of the [Habsburg Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands"), that lasted from 1556 to 1714. This region comprised most of the modern states of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") and [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"), as well as parts of northern [France](/wiki/France "France"), the southern [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), and western [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), with the capital being [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). The [Army of Flanders](/wiki/Army_of_Flanders "Army of Flanders") was given the task of defending the territory.

The **Spanish Netherlands** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Países Bajos Españoles*; [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Spaanse Nederlanden*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Pays-Bas espagnols*; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Spanische Niederlande*; historically in Spanish: *Flandes*, the name "Flanders" was used as a *[pars pro toto](/wiki/Pars_pro_toto "Pars pro toto")*)[[4]](#cite_note-4) were a collection of [States](/wiki/Imperial_States "Imperial States") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), held in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") by the [Spanish Habsburgs](/wiki/Spanish_Habsburgs "Spanish Habsburgs"), but not annexed to the [Spanish Crown](/wiki/Spanish_Crown "Spanish Crown"), thus encompassing the second period in history of the [Habsburg Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands"), that lasted from 1556 to 1714. This region comprised most of the modern states of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") and [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"), as well as parts of northern [France](/wiki/France "France"), the southern [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), and western [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), with the capital being [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). The [Army of Flanders](/wiki/Army_of_Flanders "Army of Flanders") was given the task of defending the territory.

The Imperial fiefs in the former [Burgundian Netherlands](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands") had been inherited by the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") from the extinct [House of Valois-Burgundy](/wiki/House_of_Valois-Burgundy "House of Valois-Burgundy") upon the death of [Mary of Burgundy](/wiki/Mary_of_Burgundy "Mary of Burgundy") in 1482. The [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces") formed the core of the [Habsburg Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands"), which passed to the Spanish Habsburgs upon the abdication of Emperor [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") in 1556. Spanish hegemony in Netherlands was solidified following their victory in the [Fall of Antwerp](/wiki/Fall_of_Antwerp "Fall of Antwerp") during the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War"). When part of the Netherlands separated to form the autonomous [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") in 1581, the remainder of the area stayed under Spanish rule until the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession").

### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Netherlands&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

See also: [Burgundian inheritance in the Low Countries](/wiki/Burgundian_inheritance_in_the_Low_Countries "Burgundian inheritance in the Low Countries")

A common administration of fiefs in the Low Countries, centered in the [Duchy of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant"), already existed under the rule of the Burgundian Duke [Philip the Good](/wiki/Philip_the_Good "Philip the Good") with the implementation of a [stadtholder](/wiki/Stadtholder "Stadtholder") and the first convocation of the [States General of the Netherlands](/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands "States General of the Netherlands") in 1464.[[5]](#cite_note-5) His granddaughter Mary had confirmed a number of privileges to the States by the [Great Privilege](/wiki/Great_Privilege "Great Privilege") signed in 1477.[[6]](#cite_note-6) After the government takeover by her husband Archduke [Maximilian I of Austria](/wiki/Maximilian_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Maximilian I, Holy Roman Emperor"), the States insisted on their privileges, culminating in a [Hook](/wiki/Hook_and_Cod_wars "Hook and Cod wars") rebellion in [Holland](/wiki/County_of_Holland "County of Holland") and [Flemish revolts](/wiki/Flemish_revolts_against_Maximilian_of_Austria "Flemish revolts against Maximilian of Austria"). Maximilian prevailed with the support of Duke [Albert III of Saxony](/wiki/Albert_III,_Duke_of_Saxony "Albert III, Duke of Saxony") and his son [Philip the Handsome](/wiki/Philip_I_of_Castile "Philip I of Castile"), husband of [Joanna of Castile](/wiki/Joanna_of_Castile "Joanna of Castile"), could assume the rule over the Habsburg Netherlands in 1493.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlockmansPrevenier1999206-234-7)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaemers2009-8)

Philip as well as his son and successor [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") retained the title of a "[Duke of Burgundy](/wiki/Duke_of_Burgundy "Duke of Burgundy")" referring to their Burgundian inheritance, but not having the [Duchy of Burgundy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Burgundy "Duchy of Burgundy") in their possession, since it was taken by the French already in 1477. Only the [Free County of Burgundy](/wiki/Free_County_of_Burgundy "Free County of Burgundy"), in the Holy Roman Empire, remained in Habsburg rule, since 1493. The Habsburgs often used the term *Burgundy* to refer to their hereditary lands both in historical Burgundy and the Low Countries (e.g. in the name of the Imperial [Burgundian Circle](/wiki/Burgundian_Circle "Burgundian Circle") established in 1512), actually until 1795, when the [Austrian Netherlands](/wiki/Austrian_Netherlands "Austrian Netherlands") were lost to the [French Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"). The [Governor-general of the Netherlands](/wiki/Governor-general_of_the_Netherlands "Governor-general of the Netherlands") was responsible for the administration of the Habsburg lands in the Low Countries. Charles V was born and raised in the Low Countries and often stayed at the [Palace of Coudenberg](/wiki/Palace_of_Coudenberg "Palace of Coudenberg") in Brussels.

By the [Pragmatic Sanction of 1549](/wiki/Pragmatic_Sanction_of_1549 "Pragmatic Sanction of 1549"), Charles V declared the Seventeen Provinces a united and indivisible Habsburg dominion. Between 1555 and 1556, the House of Habsburg split into an Austrian and a Spanish branch as a consequence of Charles's abdications: the Netherlands were left to his son [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"), while his brother King [Ferdinand I](/wiki/Ferdinand_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Ferdinand I, Holy Roman Emperor") succeeded him as [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor"). The Seventeen Provinces, *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* still fiefs of the Holy Roman Empire, from that time on *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* were ruled by the Spanish branch of the Habsburgs.

### Eighty Years' War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Netherlands&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Eighty Years' War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Jeton_Brabant_Antwerpen_Albert_Isabella_1612.jpg/250px-Jeton_Brabant_Antwerpen_Albert_Isabella_1612.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jeton_Brabant_Antwerpen_Albert_Isabella_1612.jpg)

Jeton with portraits of the Archdukes [Albert VII, Archduke of Austria](/wiki/Albert_VII,_Archduke_of_Austria "Albert VII, Archduke of Austria") and [Infanta Isabella of Spain](/wiki/Isabella_Clara_Eugenia "Isabella Clara Eugenia"), struck in Antwerp 1612.  
Obv: Portraits of Albert and Isabella.   
Rev: Eagle holding balance, date 1612.

Main article: [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War")

See also: [Army of Flanders](/wiki/Army_of_Flanders "Army of Flanders")

Philip's stern [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation") measures sparked the [Dutch Revolt](/wiki/Dutch_Revolt "Dutch Revolt") in the mainly [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") Netherlandish provinces, which led to the outbreak of the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") in 1568. In January 1579 the seven northern provinces formed the Protestant [Union of Utrecht](/wiki/Union_of_Utrecht "Union of Utrecht"), which declared independence from the Spanish branch of the Habsburgs as the [Republic of the Seven United Netherlands](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") by the 1581 [Act of Abjuration](/wiki/Act_of_Abjuration "Act of Abjuration"). The Spanish branch of the Habsburgs could retain the rule only over the partly Catholic [Southern Netherlands](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands"), completed after the [Fall of Antwerp](/wiki/Fall_of_Antwerp "Fall of Antwerp") in 1585.

Better times came, when in 1598 the Spanish Netherlands passed to Philip's daughter [Isabella Clara Eugenia](/wiki/Isabella_Clara_Eugenia "Isabella Clara Eugenia") and her husband Archduke [Albert VII of Austria](/wiki/Albert_VII,_Archduke_of_Austria "Albert VII, Archduke of Austria"). The couple's rule brought a period of much-needed peace and stability to the economy, which stimulated the growth of a separate South Netherlandish identity and consolidated the authority of the House of Habsburg reconciling previous anti-Spanish sentiments. In the early 17th century, there was a flourishing court at [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). Among the artists who emerged from the court of the "Archdukes", as they were known, was [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens"). Under Isabella and Albert, the Spanish Netherlands actually had formal independence from Spain, but always remained unofficially within the Spanish sphere of influence. With Albert's death in 1621 they returned to formal Spanish control, although the childless Isabella remained on as governor until her death in 1633.

The failing wars intended to regain the 'heretical' northern Netherlands meant significant loss of (still mainly Catholic) territories in the north, which was consolidated in 1648 in the [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia"), and given the peculiar inferior status of *[Generality Lands](/wiki/Generality_Lands "Generality Lands")* (jointly ruled by the United Republic, not admitted as member provinces): [Zeelandic Flanders](/wiki/Zeelandic_Flanders "Zeelandic Flanders") (south of the River [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt")), the present Dutch province of [North Brabant](/wiki/North_Brabant "North Brabant") and [Maastricht](/wiki/Maastricht "Maastricht") (in the present-day Dutch province of [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Netherlands) "Limburg (Netherlands)")).

### French conquests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Netherlands&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: French conquests")]

As the power of the Spanish branch of the Habsburgs waned in the latter decades of the 17th century, the territory of the Netherlands under Habsburg rule was repeatedly invaded by the French and an increasing portion of the territory came under French control in successive wars. By the [Treaty of the Pyrenees](/wiki/Treaty_of_the_Pyrenees "Treaty of the Pyrenees") of 1659 the French annexed most of [Artois](/wiki/County_of_Artois "County of Artois"), and [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk,_France "Dunkirk, France") was ceded to the English. By the [Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle](/wiki/Treaty_of_Aix-la-Chapelle_(1668) "Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1668)") (ending the [War of Devolution](/wiki/War_of_Devolution "War of Devolution") in 1668) and [Nijmegen](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nijmegen "Treaty of Nijmegen") (ending the [Franco-Dutch War](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War") in 1678), further territory up to the current Franco-Belgian border was ceded, including [Cambrai](/wiki/Cambrai "Cambrai"), [Walloon Flanders](/wiki/Walloon_Flanders "Walloon Flanders"), as well as [half of the County of Hainaut](/wiki/French_Hainaut "French Hainaut") (including [Valenciennes](/wiki/Valenciennes "Valenciennes")), and further to the south Spanish Habsburgs also lost the [Free County of Burgundy](/wiki/Free_County_of_Burgundy "Free County of Burgundy").[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDee200964-65-9) Later, in the [War of the Reunions](/wiki/War_of_the_Reunions "War of the Reunions") and the [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War"), France annexed other parts of the region that were restored to Spain by the [Treaty of Rijswijk](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rijswijk "Treaty of Rijswijk") 1697.

During the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession"), in 1706 the Habsburg Netherlands became an Anglo-Dutch [condominium](/wiki/Condominium_(international_law) "Condominium (international law)") for the remainder of the conflict.[[10]](#cite_note-10) By the peace treaties of [Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") and [Rastatt](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rastatt "Treaty of Rastatt") in 1713/14 ending the war, the Southern Netherlands returned to the Austrian [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") forming the [Austrian Netherlands](/wiki/Austrian_Netherlands "Austrian Netherlands").

## Provinces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Netherlands&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Provinces")]

From 1581 the Habsburg Netherlands consisted of the following territories, all part of modern Belgium unless otherwise stated:

1. the [Duchy of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant"), except for [North Brabant](/wiki/North_Brabant "North Brabant") part of the [Generality Lands](/wiki/Generality_Lands "Generality Lands") of the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") in 1648, including the former [Margraviate of Antwerp](/wiki/Margraviate_of_Antwerp "Margraviate of Antwerp") (now mostly Belgium, some in Netherlands)
2. the [Duchy of Limburg](/wiki/Duchy_of_Limburg "Duchy of Limburg"), except for Limburg of the States part of the Dutch Generality Lands from 1648
3. the [Duchy of Luxembourg](/wiki/Duchy_of_Luxembourg "Duchy of Luxembourg"), a sovereign state from 1815 ([parts](/wiki/Partitions_of_Luxembourg "Partitions of Luxembourg") in modern Belgium, France and Germany)
4. the [Upper Quarter](/wiki/Upper_Guelders "Upper Guelders") (*Bovenkwartier*) of the [Duchy of Guelders](/wiki/Duchy_of_Guelders "Duchy of Guelders") (Now Netherlands and Germany: the area around [Venlo](/wiki/Venlo "Venlo") and [Roermond](/wiki/Roermond "Roermond"), in the present Dutch province of [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Netherlands) "Limburg (Netherlands)"), and the town of [Geldern](/wiki/Geldern "Geldern") in the present German district of [Kleve](/wiki/Kleve_(district) "Kleve (district)"))
5. the [County of Artois](/wiki/County_of_Artois "County of Artois"), ceded to [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") by the 1659 [Treaty of the Pyrenees](/wiki/Treaty_of_the_Pyrenees "Treaty of the Pyrenees") (now in France)
6. the [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders"), except for [Zeelandic Flanders](/wiki/Zeelandic_Flanders "Zeelandic Flanders") part of the Dutch Generality Lands from 1648, [Walloon Flanders](/wiki/Walloon_Flanders "Walloon Flanders") ceded to France by the 1678 [Peace of Nijmegen](/wiki/Treaties_of_Nijmegen "Treaties of Nijmegen") (now in Belgium and France [French Flanders](/wiki/French_Flanders "French Flanders"))
7. the [County of Namur](/wiki/County_of_Namur "County of Namur")
8. the [County of Hainaut](/wiki/County_of_Hainaut "County of Hainaut"), southern part with [Valenciennes](/wiki/Valenciennes "Valenciennes") ceded to France by the 1678 Peace of Nijmegen (now in Belgium and France)
9. the [Lordship of Mechelen](/wiki/Lordship_of_Mechelen "Lordship of Mechelen")[[note 1]](#cite_note-11)
10. the [Tournaisis](/wiki/Tournaisis "Tournaisis")
11. the Prince-Bishopric of Cambrai (the [Cambrésis](/wiki/Cambr%C3%A9sis "Cambrésis")), not part of the Seventeen Provinces, incorporated by King Philip II in 1559, ceded to France by the 1678 Peace of Nijmegen (now in France: roughly the central part of the [département](/wiki/D%C3%A9partement "Département") [Nord](/wiki/Nord_(d%C3%A9partement) "Nord (département)"))
